item.title: How Did Josef Mengele Evade Capture For 34 Years?
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In 1985, investigators in Brazil dug up a body that had lain undisturbed for 6 years. It was Josef Mengele - the Nazi 'Angel of Death'. As Auschwitz camp physician, Mengele's sadism and perverse obsession with racial purity saw no bounds. After the war, he escaped justice in Buenos Aires. But Wiesenthal was on his tail. He was nearly caught several times; in an allied POW camp after the war; in possession of suspicious papers in an Italian jail; twice using his real name inside Argentina's German Embassy. But what eventually happened to one of the most evil men on earth?

item.title: Loneliness and AI - In love with a life-sized doll | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Chris lives in North Carolina (USA) with his girlfriend Mimi, a life-size sex doll. He communicates with her using an AI app. A clear-eyed, fascinating look at AI and its social consequences.
Chris, a petrol station attendant in his 30s, lives with his girlfriend Mimi, a life-size love doll, in a small town in North Carolina. He communicates with Mimi via an AI app. He talks about his childhood and the traumas he has experienced. Despite his complicated and painful family history, Chris has developed into a kind, friendly man. The film makes no secret of his fascination with (or even fetishization of) media featuring men with robot women. But above all, Chris is a deeply lonely person; a romantic who dreams of true love.
An honest, candid and fascinating take on machine learning and AI, rising levels of loneliness and the social implications for people turning to technology for a sense of security and to gain control over their circumstances.

item.title: 'The Whole Nine Yards' Myth: Debunked by Firearms Expert Jonathan Ferguson
item.author: Royal Armouries
item.content:encoded:
Why do people say “the whole nine yards”… and what does it actually mean?
In this special spin-off of What Is This Weapon? Jonathan explores one of the most persistent myths in firearms history, the idea that the phrase comes from aircraft machine gun ammunition belts.
00:00 Intro 00:15 Exploring Myths, Folklore and Firearms 00:25 The Phrase “The Whole Nine Yards” 00:48 The Browning .50 cal and Aircraft Guns 01:17 Popular Theories 03:41 Measuring Ammunition Belts (Does it Add Up?) 05:29 Multiple Guns Problem 07:41 Earlier Uses of the Phrase (Pre-Aircraft) 10:39 How the Phrase Likely Spread 11:32 Final Verdict & Outro
Sources/Reading for Whole Nine Yards;
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_whole_nine_yards (yes, really! all other sources on this are out of date or just wrong)
*editor note* Cue meme of Henry Cavil stating his dissappointment of using wikipedia as a source of information)
Original for 'The Judge's Shirt' - https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=7JFRAAAAYAAJ&dq=%22what%20a%20silly%20stupid%20woman!%22&pg=PA163#v=onepage&q&f=false
'The Stupid Boy' (in which a mother sends a boy out to sell three yards of cloth at a time - no telling if this might pre-date The Judge's Shirt but it wasn't collected until the 1880s); https://archive.org/details/folklorejournal06folkuoft/page/184/
Article reference: Jonathan Ferguson & Terence Smith '"One in the breech, five in the magazine”: British Aircrew Armament of the First World War', ARMAX: The Journal of Contemporary Arms Vol. VIII No.I 2022. (Back issue available at https://www.headstamppublishing.com/purchase/p/armax-vol-viii-no-1-springsummer-2022)

item.title: Why Does This WW2 Painting Have a Naked Guy on the Back?
item.author: Imperial War Museums
item.content:encoded:
These paintings have a secret. Something that our visitors won’t get a chance to see. These extraordinary works by Henry Carr, Bernard Hailstone, and Joan Vernon Connew have paint on both sides of the canvass.
In this video we examine how were these paintings were created, why they have got works on both sides, and what these works can tell us about being an artist during the Second World War.
CHAPTERS
0:00 Why Are These Paintings Double Sided? 1:28 How Were They Created? 3:35 What Is On The Front? 5:49 What Is On The Back? 7:00 How Were They Conserved? 8:23 What Do They Tell Us?

item.title: The German Princess Who Staged A Coup D'état For The Russian Throne
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
At the age of 14 Sophie Auguste Friederike von Anhalt-Zerbst, a princess from a minor German aristocratic family journeyed to Russia. Discover the unbelievable true story of Catherine the Great of Russia. Born a minor German princess, she endured a difficult marriage to Grand Duke Peter, survived court intrigue, and eventually orchestrated a daring coup d'état to seize the throne, becoming the greatest female ruler in Russian history. Uncover the political maneuvers, scandalous love life, and ultimate ambition that transformed her from a foreign bride into the absolute Empress of Russia.

item.title: Cyberattacks, data encryption, extortion - How cybercriminals operate | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Ransomware poses an existential threat to businesses. It's a form of cybercrime that's a highly professional industry. The criminals operate like a business - by infiltrating systems, encrypting data, and extorting money.
Those who refuse to pay, face operational shutdown, loss of data, and bankruptcy. Many criminal groups have a division of labor that's similar to that of companies, with teams of developers, negotiators, 'customer support,' and clear deadlines. This documentary shows how almost any company can be targeted and how quickly an attack can threaten the very survival of the business. In the spring of 2025, Philipp Bosshard’s company was hit. It wasn't just the IT system that went down, the entire company ground to a halt. The future of the business and the jobs of its 200 employees were at stake. The hackers set a deadline: Bosshard had twelve days to decide whether to pay the ransom or see if he could somehow find another solution.
The documentary also tracks the cybercriminals themselves. Through months of undercover investigations, cybersecurity analyst Jon DiMaggio managed to infiltrate one of the most notorious ransomware gangs: Lockbit. He even engaged with the group's mysterious leader, who went by the name "LockbitSupp" - befriending him and ultimately unmasking his identity.
When Lockbit itself was hacked in 2025, the inner workings of the gang were laid bare. Data journalist Simon Huwiler analyzed the leaked data and stumbled on one unusually large ransom payment, with a trail leading to Switzerland...

item.title: How do snakes swallow animals so much bigger than they are? - Niko Zlotnik
item.author: TED-Ed
item.content:encoded:
Explore how snakes evolved, and find out how their jaw-dropping anatomy allows them to take down prey several times their size.
--
Since slithering onto the scene some 150 million years ago, evolving length and limbless-ness out of their ancestral lizard forms, snakes have diversified rapidly. Their noodly bodies and flexible heads granted them access to novel places and prey. And today, there are nearly 4,000 snake species, spanning habitats high and low. Niko Zlotnik explores how snakes evolved into incredible predators.
Lesson by Niko Zlotnik, directed by Anna Benner.

item.title: Finding Vinland: Archeological Evidence Of Vikings Landing In The US
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
Did the Vikings reach the heart of America 500 years before Columbus? Join forensic geologist Scott Wolter as he investigates the Heavener Runestone in Oklahoma and a dangerous, bomb-laden island off Massachusetts to uncover evidence of Norse exploration. From ancient runes to geological weathering, this documentary explores the breadcrumb trail of artifacts that suggest the Viking Sagas didn't end in Canada, but continued deep into the United States.

item.title: The Dark Side Of The Freemasons: Unmasking 600-Years Of Secret Brotherhood
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
00:00 The Freemasons Dive into the captivating history of the Freemasons, one of the world's oldest and most secretive organizations. This documentary traces their journey from the stonemason guilds of medieval England to their profound influence on revolutions, politics, and conspiracy theories. We explore the symbolism, rituals, and historical figures—from George Washington to Albert Pike—that define the brotherhood. Discover the truth behind the myths, from the anti-Masonic panic of the 19th century to their conflict with Nazi Germany, and understand the complex legacy of an organization that has shaped our world for centuries.
43:34 Skull And Bones Step inside the secretive world of Skull and Bones, Yale University's most notorious secret society. This documentary explores the history and myths behind the "Tomb," the society's exclusive headquarters. We uncover the truth behind its strange initiation rituals, famous members like the Bush family, and the persistent conspiracy theories that claim this elite group secretly controls America. Is it an harmless college club or a powerful network of puppet masters? Join us as we pull back the curtain on one of the country's most enduring mysteries.
01:27:08 The Hermetic Order Of The Golden Dawn The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn emerged in late 19th-century London, capitalizing on a spiritual void created by the Industrial Revolution. Founded by William Wescott, William Robert Woodman, and Samuel Liddell MacGregor Mathers, the secret society claimed its authority came from mysterious "Cipher Manuscripts" and the "Secret Chiefs," ascended beings who provided them with ancient knowledge. The order's curriculum, a blend of various mystical traditions like Hermetic Qabalah and alchemy, attracted many notable figures, including poet W.B. Yeats, and was unique for its inclusion of women. However, the society's internal rivalries, particularly a power struggle involving its most infamous member, Aleister Crowley, led to its eventual demise by 1939. Despite its short lifespan, the Golden Dawn's legacy continues to influence modern occultism, with key figures shaping our understanding of magic and mysticism.

item.title: From Syria to Germany - Nour’s dream to become a doctor | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
It was 2015 when Nour Elhouda Al Ahmad Alhammash and her family arrived in eastern Germany. After fleeing Syria, the then nine-year-old was enrolled in the first grade at a school in the village of Golzow, Brandenburg.
In the summer of 2015, Nour Elhouda Al Ahmad Alhammash, the child of a refugee family from Damascus, started school in the village of Golzow. The mayor of Golzow wanted to save the village's primary school from closure, but there weren't enough enrolments. He saw the integration of refugee families as an opportunity for the village. But local people were unsure.
Nour was a fast learner, quickly picking up the German language and skipping two grades. In 2019, she moved up to secondary school. In 2025, she took her secondary school leaving exams, or "Abitur.” Her goal: to study medicine. Nour now lives with her family in Frankfurt (Oder), where her father works as a tiler. The family has welcomed another child and has no plans to return to Syria for now.
A team of reporters have been accompanying Nour Alhammash and her family since their arrival in Germany. The documentary tells a story of courage, ambition and new beginnings.

item.title: Octopuses have nine brains? | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
The plural of octopus isn’t “octopi,” it’s actually octopuses. But that’s far from the most fascinating thing about them. Octopuses are incredibly smart and well adapted for survival. They’ve been around for over 330 million years, long before dinosaurs roamed the Earth, giving them a huge evolutionary head start. Together with their squid and cuttlefish relatives, octopuses have developed camouflage skills that seem out of this world. Their skin is covered with thousands of color-changing cells called chromatophores, tiny sacs filled with different pigments that expand and contract to change color. To process all this sensory input, octopuses have evolved remarkably. As well as the central brain in their head, each of their eight arms has its own mini brain, which can sense, move, and react independently.
Watch “Octopus under threat - Searching for clues around the Mediterranean” on the DW Documentary YouTube channel.

item.title: Haiti - The true cost of independence | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Haiti gained independence from France in 1804 after a rebellion by the enslaved population. However, the French government forced the young state to make drastic financial reparations to its former colonists.
On 1 January 1804, Haitian slaves achieved the unimaginable. They drove out the French colonists and declared their independence. Two centuries later, Haiti is one of the poorest countries in the world. Endemic corruption and a failing state are often blamed for the disaster. However, Haiti's ongoing misfortune is largely due to its colonial history and its relationship with France. After declaring independence, Haiti faced relentless pressure from France, which sought to regain control of the Caribbean territory. In 1825, France waived its claim in exchange for an indemnity of 150 million gold francs. This was an oppressive burden intended to compensate the former colonists for the slaves and land they had lost. It took Haiti more than a hundred years to settle the debt.
This film examines how this forced debt plunged Haiti into a long-term cycle of poverty, and prevented the Caribbean nation from developing into a flourishing nation. Who profited from this punitive agreement? Has Haiti been plundered by foreign powers, foreign banks and its own leaders since its foundation?

item.title: What Were The Final Months Inside Auschwitz-Birkenau Like?
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In 1944, the Nazi extermination machine reached its most efficient and terrifying peak. This documentary explores the final year of Auschwitz-Birkenau, focusing on the mass deportation of Hungarian Jews and the desperate efforts of the SS to hide their crimes as the Red Army approached. Featuring haunting survivor testimony, we uncover the reality of the railway ramp, the secret photographs smuggled out by the Sonderkommando, and the brutal "Death Marches" that preceded liberation. This is the definitive account of the Holocaust’s darkest hour.

item.title: Gothic architecture - When cathedrals reached up to the heavens | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Gothic architecture emerged in France in the 12th century. This architectural style revolutionized church construction, creating cathedrals that soared skywards. But where did the knowledge and technology for building these monuments come from?
Light floods the interiors of Gothic cathedrals, which have colorful windows instead of solid walls. All told, the windows of Chartres Cathedral in France would cover an area larger than a football pitch. Another impressive example is St. Stephen's Cathedral in Vienna, Austria, the federal capital's iconic landmark.
This documentary attempts to unravel the mysteries and secrets of Gothic architecture. Ribbed vaults, pointed arches and flying buttresses became integral elements of the Gothic style. The mysticism of light inspired architects to install large windows that illuminated the interior and made visiting cathedrals a truly enlightening experience. Robust Romanesque construction gives way to the graceful, soaring architectural elements of Gothic style. What secrets lie in the statics of the elongated columns, struts and pillars?
The answers can be found in this documentary, which features experts like Barbara Schock-Werner, former master builder of Cologne Cathedral.
The film also traces the lives of famous Gothic architects. They were universal geniuses, masters of architecture but also of physics, mathematics and chemistry. They kept their knowledge a closely guarded secret to maintain their monopoly and professional status.

item.title: Cows: Saviors of the Alps | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Almost 40% of the planet is covered by natural grasslands. In places like the Alps, these landscapes are a "desert" for humans. Why? Because we can’t eat grass, and the steep slopes make plowing for crops impossible. Plowing here wouldn't just be hard—it would trigger devastating soil erosion. This is where the cow becomes a biological catalyst. They possess a unique superpower: they transform the "useless" cellulose in grass into high-quality protein. But it’s not just about food. As they graze, they stimulate plants to grow deeper roots, turning the soil into a massive carbon sink.
Watch “The fate of bull calves - From surplus to Alpine savior” on the DW Documentary YouTube channel.

item.title: Something Disturbing Happens When You Solve Einstein's Equations This Way
item.author: PBS Space Time
item.content:encoded:
Check out the Universe in a Black Hole Merch at the Space Time Merch Store https://www.pbsspacetime.com/shop
Kurt Gödel discovered a solution to General Relativity that allows time travel without any exotic physics, revealing that the theory doesn’t actually guarantee a consistent chain of cause and effect . His “Gödel universe” shows that under certain conditions, the structure of spacetime itself can loop back on itself—blurring the line between past and future and exposing a deep limitation in our understanding of reality.

item.title: Abolish prisons? - What works better than incarceration | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Approximately 58,000 people are currently incarcerated in German prisons. Over half of them reoffend after their release. These statistics raise doubts about the prison system. Is something fundamentally wrong with German prisons?
To understand what prison does to people, journalist Frank Seibert voluntarily goes to jail. For a few hours, a cell door closes behind him. Isolation and the sudden loss of control change everything, he says after his "release".
What good does imprisonment do? Lawyer and former prison governor Thomas Galli says: nothing! He would release 90 per cent of people behind bars on parole. Prison means social exclusion. But it is also expensive, costing the state some €200 per prisoner, per day. In total, this means the system costs taxpayers over €10 million - every single day. Thomas Galli would rather see that money invested in social work and therapy for offenders.
René Müller has been working as a prison officer for over 20 years and disagrees. Locking up criminals serves the purposes of general safety, deterrence and a sense of justice, he says.
Frank Seibert explores the question: Who is right? To find answers, he meets with criminal law expert Kristin Drenkhahn from the Free University of Berlin, who is investigating the effect of the social climate in prisons on rehabilitation. Former prisoner Thomas, who attempted to kill a woman, describes this atmosphere from a very personal perspective. Prison changes people - but not for the better, in most cases, he concludes.
Neuroscientist Simone Kühn from the Max Planck Institute in Berlin and psychiatrist Johannes Fuß from the University of Duisburg/Essen are investigating the behavior and associated brain changes of prisoners. This is the world's first research project to investigate whether life behind bars is so monotonous and lacking in stimulation that certain regions of the brain shrink or even become inactive. Does prison make people sick, rather than better fit for life after their sentence ends?
In Norway, the then Minister of Justice asked himself this question back in the late 1980s, when recidivism rates were alarmingly high. To answer it, he convened a group of experts. Lawyer Are Høidal was one of its members. Frank Seibert meets Høidal in a prison near Stavanger. This prison operates according to what’s known as the ‘normality principle.’ This means no one serves a sentence under stricter circumstances than necessary for the security in the community, and that all criminals are treated with respect and kindness. Prisoners who cooperate - and that is most of them - are allowed to move to a residential area after a short time. They live here in small communities, without prison bars.
[Warning: This documentary addresses the topic of suicide. If you are suffering from serious emotional strain or suicidal thoughts, do not hesitate to seek professional help. Details of support available in many countries can be found at Befrienders Worldwide: https://www.befrienders.org/ ]

item.title: The haunting history of the Paris Catacombs - Stephanie H. Smith
item.author: TED-Ed
item.content:encoded:
Take a tour of the vast, underground network of the Paris Catacombs, and find out how this 18th century cemetery came to be.
--
For centuries, Parisians had buried their dead in the Cemetery of the Innocents. By the 18th century, the grounds had become overcrowded — spewing foul odors across the city. Residents worried that it posed a public health threat, and petitioned the government for a solution. But where could they move millions of remains? Stephanie H. Smith explores the vast, underground network of the Catacombs.
Lesson by Stephanie H. Smith, directed by Laura Jayne Hodkin.

item.title: The Bizarre Behaviour Of Rotating Bodies
item.author: Veritasium
item.content:encoded:
What you are looking at is known as the Dzhanibekov effect or the tennis racket theorem or the intermediate axis theorem.
It involves arguably the best mathematician alive, Soviet era secrets, and the end of the world.
So in 1985, cosmonaut Vladimir Dzhanibekov was tasked with saving the Soviet space station Salyut 7 which had completely shut down. The mission was so dramatic that the Russians made a movie out of it in 2017 and after rescuing the space station, Dzhanibekov unpacked supplies sent up from Earth which were locked down with a wing-nut and as the wing-nut spun off the bolt, he noticed something strange:
The wing-nut maintained its orientation for a short time, and then it flipped, 180 degrees. And as he kept watching, it flipped back a few seconds later and it continued flipping back and forth at regular intervals.
This motion wasn’t caused by forces or torques applied to the wing-nut: there were none. And yet it kept flipping. It was a strange and counterintuitive phenomenon. One that the Russians kept secret for 10 years.

item.title: What Was Daily Life Like Inside Auschwitz-Birkenau?
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In this moving documentary, survivors of the Auschwitz-Birkenau complex share their harrowing experiences of life inside the most notorious death camp in history. From the construction of the gas chambers to the brutal "Kapos" system and the terrifying daily selections, these first-hand accounts reveal the extreme measures taken to survive. Discover the true story of the "Builder School," the role of the camp orchestras, and the desperate resistance efforts that took place under the eyes of the SS. This is the definitive look at the daily reality of the Holocaust.

item.title: The Da Vinci Code - Has the mystery been solved? | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Dan Brown's novel "The Da Vinci Code” is one of the century’s most successful thrillers worldwide. But how much truth is behind the claims made in the book - and what is pure fiction?
The novel "The Da Vinci Code” by American author Dan Brown has sold around 80 million copies since its publication in 2003. The film adaptation starring Tom Hanks in the lead role was also a global success. Thematically, the thriller moves between art, mysticism, religion, and conspiracy theories.
But the theories presented in both the book and the film adaptation also drew criticism, especially from the Catholic Church. Some religious groups even called for a boycott.
This documentary delves deep into the history behind the novel's most controversial claims.
Could a secret bloodline linked to Jesus have survived for centuries, hidden by powerful forces? Did Leonardo encode these secrets in his famous painting "The Last Supper,” and other works?
With the help of leading experts in art history, religious studies, and medieval conspiracy theories, as well as insights from the 2006 film adaptation, the documentary unravels fact and fiction. The truth, it seems, might be more astonishing than fiction.

item.title: Italy’s young farmers | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
A growing shift is reshaping Italy’s countryside. Data from the Italian farmers’ association shows that increasing numbers of young people are leaving cities behind to work in agriculture. Many of them are making a deliberate mid-career change. In the rugged hills of Genoa, Davide and Matteo are part of this movement. With no inheritance and no guarantees, they’re building something from the ground up, driven by the pull of a life closer to nature. This is the face of Italy’s new agricultural revolution.
Watch “Organic farming as a career choice: Italy’s young farmers” on the DW Documentary YouTube channel.

item.title: Secrets Of The National Maritime Museum: The Dark Truth Behind Britain's Navy
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In this deep dive into the National Maritime Museum, we reveal the grisly truths behind Britain’s greatest naval legends. Discover the ballistics behind the shot that killed Admiral Nelson, the chemical secrets of the Russian "infernal machine" sea mines, and the harrowing forensic trail of the lost Franklin Expedition. From the Mutiny on the Bounty to modern-day piracy, we explore the innovations and tragedies that shaped the high seas. This is the history of the Royal Navy as you’ve never seen it before.

item.title: Slovakia divided - Between Russia and Europe | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Slovakia stands at a crossroads between western and eastern Europe - and not just in geographical terms. Amidst growing social and political divisions in the country, Prime Minister Robert Fico is using his power to amplify tensions and stir up fear.
Once a mainstream Social Democrat, Robert Fico has reinvented himself as a leftwing nationalist - repeatedly attacking the media while pursuing friendly relations with Moscow and stalling EU sanctions against Russia. With confidence in the government and public institutions steadily eroding, many young people are leaving the country - while others take to the streets to fight for democratic values and strengthening Slovakia's ties to the EU.
Located at an altitude of 2,250 meters, Chata pod Rysmi is the highest mountain lodge in Slovakia. It's run by Viktor Beránek: not just a warm-hearted host but also a legendary sherpa - and a vocal critic of populist prime minister Robert Fico. The country's longest-serving mountain hut manager has now lost his lease after almost half a century, likely due to his political positions. He's now considering his homeland.
Down in the liberal-minded capital Bratislava, locals gather regularly to protest - against sweeping interventions in the judiciary, government pressure on the media, and attempts to align the country closer to Russia while the prime minister makes friendly visits to Moscow. Peter Bárdy, editor-in-chief at the Aktuality.sk news portal, has been following these developments and says they are driven not by ideology but rather business interests, cronyism and the pursuit - and preservation - of power.
On the other side of the political divide are people like 22-year-old Terezka Mičianiková (from the town of Trnava), who believes in a strong government that puts Slovakia first. In her eyes, the EU intervenes too heavily in domestic issues and is a far bigger source of problems than Fico. The law student and influencer wants to see a confident and more nationally-oriented Slovakia - maintaining a clear distance from Brussels.
Meanwhile, a delegation from the European Parliament traveled to Bratislava to gain a first-hand impression of the judiciary, media freedom and anti-corruption measures. Slovakia is increasingly being called the EU's new "problem child", as its government continues to question the EU's values and expectations.
#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs ______

item.title: What happens if you eat a silica gel packet? - Vivian Jiang
item.author: TED-Ed
item.content:encoded:
Explore the science of silica gel, and find out why these packets are everywhere and if they're actually dangerous to ingest.
--
Of the many, many things we’re not supposed to snack on, perhaps the most common forbidden food is silica gel. This tiny packet pops up in packaging for everything — from TVs and guitar cases to sunscreen and snack foods. So why is silica gel everywhere, and what would actually happen if you ate it? Vivian Jiang digs into the science of this pervasive product.
Lesson by Vivian Jiang, directed by Denys Spolitak.

item.title: The $5 Billion Plan to Rebuild Downtown Atlanta
item.author: Bloomberg Originals
item.content:encoded:
Downtown Atlanta was once the center of the city’s cultural and economic life, but decades of decline left much of it underused. Now, a $5 billion redevelopment project known as Centennial Yards aims to transform a 50 acre site called the Gulch into a new hub for housing, entertainment, and business.
With the 2026 FIFA World Cup approaching and billions at stake, the project raises a central question: can this effort restore lasting vitality to the heart of Atlanta?
An NBA Owner, Music Stars and Athletes Team Up for a $5 Billion Atlanta Bet: https://www.bloomberg.com/features/2026-atlanta-centennial-yards/
The Centennial Yards project aims to transform a long-stalled site, with a coalition that leaned on local Black leaders to get it done.

item.title: Uncensored Footage From The My Lai Massacre: Vietnam War's Darkest Day
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In March 1968, the young soldiers of Charlie Company entered the village of My Lai. Four hours later, hundreds of innocent civilians were dead. This documentary investigates the My Lai Massacre, one of the most haunting chapters of the Vietnam War. Featuring raw interviews with veterans who were there, we examine the brutal training, the breakdown of command, and the lifelong psychological scars left on those who pulled the trigger.

item.title: The Most Insane Megaproject You Never Heard About
item.author: Kurzgesagt – In a Nutshell
item.content:encoded:
Do you have a balanced news diet? Go to https://ground.news/nutshell to see reporting from a variety of sources and perspectives around the world. Subscribe for 40% off their unlimited access Vantage plan through our link.
Visit https://www.youtube.com/@nightshift_kurzgesagt and explore the weird, beautiful, and sometimes unsettling side of history.
Sources & further reading: https://sites.google.com/view/sources-plowshare/
At the height of the Cold War, nuclear weapons were seen not only as devices of destruction, but also as tools for progress. Project Plowshare was a bold attempt to use atomic explosions for more practical purposes: from digging canals and creating harbors to reshaping entire landscapes. This project was designed to push the limits of what seemed possible, but instead turned into an environmental disaster.
How did Project Plowshare come up? What happened to it, and why did it fail in the end?

item.title: The Final Months Of WW2 | The End Of The War In Color
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
0:00:00 - Unconditional Surrender: Relive the monumental events of May 1945 as Hollywood director George Stevens captures Germany's unconditional surrender and the dawn of peace in Europe. This rare color footage showcases the celebrations of VE Day, the liberation of Pilsen by American forces, and the devastating impact of the war on German cities like Hamburg. Witness the historic moments that marked the definitive end of World War 2 in Europe.
0:42:24 - Victors And The Defeated: We look at the aftermath of World War II in Berlin in July 1945, from the destruction of Berlin and the Allied occupation to the struggles of Berliners - especially women - to rebuild their lives amid ruins, rationing, and uncertainty about the city's future. This episode also covers the Soviet victory parade in Moscow and the tense beginning of Allied negotiations at the Potsdam Conference to decide Germany's and Europe's fate.

item.title: Archeologists Investigate The World's Best Preserved Mummy
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
Was it a holy sacrifice or a brutal crime? For decades, the Tollund Man was seen as a willing victim of Iron Age rituals. Now, a US homicide detective and leading scientists use 3D scanning and isotope analysis to challenge history. By examining the "murder weapon" still around his neck and his final meal, they uncover a story of tribal outsiders, mob justice, and a 2,400-year-old hate crime. Discover the secrets of the world’s best-preserved mummy in this gripping archaeological investigation.

item.title: What do we have to do with stardust? | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Before Earth existed, there was only dust and gas left behind by ancient stars. Over time, that material gathered together to form our planet. The same elements that once made those stars now make up most of our bodies – a quiet reminder of where we come from.
Watch “The story of our planet, retold” on the DW Documentary YouTube channel.

item.title: Auschwitz Survivors Recount Arriving At Europe's Biggest Death Camp
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In this profound documentary, survivors of Auschwitz-Birkenau recount the terrifying moments of their arrival in German-occupied Poland. From the chaotic selection process on the train platforms to the systematic dehumanization of the "quarantine" blocks, these first-hand accounts reveal the sheer scale of the Holocaust. Discover how a few survived through luck, skill, or a whispered warning, while thousands were sent to the gas chambers within hours.

item.title: Octopus under threat - Searching for clues around the Mediterranean | DW Documentary
item.author: DW Documentary
item.content:encoded:
Octopus is considered a delicacy - grilled, boiled, or fried. But rising demand has consequences, and in several regions of Europe, this intelligent cephalopod is already considered overfished.
Stocks are shrinking dramatically, especially in the Mediterranean. In Greece, illegal traps are often used for fishing. Together with the coast guard, the environmental organization Sea Shepherd has removed thousands of these traps from the seabed - part of a mission against environmental crime.
The film crew embarks on an investigative journey along the coasts of Europe and Africa. In Galicia, northwestern Spain, there are several large fish processing plants that cut up octopuses, freeze them, and prepare them for the international market. Fishing is subject to strict regulations because natural stocks are shrinking rapidly. To meet the high demand, factories are increasingly resorting to imports from African waters.
A corporation is planning to breed octopuses in aquaculture. However, the plan has been met with fierce criticism from environmentalists, and the project has been put on hold for the time being. The breeding of octopuses is ethically and ecologically questionable: the animals are highly intelligent and sensitive, suffer from stress in captivity, and no humane slaughter methods exist. In addition, their meat-based diet puts strain on marine ecosystems, and thus goes against sustainable aquaculture.
In the Mediterranean, the common octopus (octopus vulgaris) is caught in many places. In Greece, for example, long ropes of plastic traps attached to buoys are placed on the seabed. A local fisherman shows the camera team how the yield is steadily declining - a direct consequence of overfishing.
The ecological consequences of octopus overfishing are already visible: in Italy, the invasive blue crab is spreading uncontrollably. It is one of the octopus's favorite foods - but without natural enemies, it is now destroying mussel farming in the Po Delta and thus posing a threat to livelihoods. Could the return of octopuses be the solution? An organization from Austria is testing exactly that, off the Croatian island of Krk. There, researchers are investigating whether octopuses can settle in artificial caves, reproduce, and contribute to the long-term stabilization of the ecosystem. The experiments are still in their infancy, but hopes are high: a sustainable model for the entire Mediterranean region could emerge.

item.title: Inside The Scotland Yard Files: Reopening The Ash Vale Murder Case
item.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
item.content:encoded:
In 1949, James John Alcott was a soldier destined for the gallows -until a Royal pardon changed everything. This documentary explores the dark history of the man who was given a second chance, only to commit one of Britain’s most brutal railway crimes. Through expert analysis and historical records, we investigate the 1952 murder of Jeffrey "Dixie" Dean at Ash Vale station, the forensic trail that led Scotland Yard to a local boarding house, and the controversial legacy of a killer who forced a nation to rethink the ultimate penalty.
